paper_title: Vividh-ASR: A Complexity-Tiered Benchmark and Optimization Dynamics for Robust Indic Speech Recognition

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Fine-tuning multilingual ASR models like Whisper for low-resource languages often improves read speech but degrades spontaneous audio performance, a phenomenon we term studio-bias. To diagnose this mismatch, we introduce Vividh-ASR, a complexity-stratified benchmark for Hindi and Malayalam across four tiers: studio, broadcast, spontaneous, and synthetic noise. Through a controlled study of learning-rate timing and curriculum ordering, we find that early large parameter updates improve global WER by 12 absolute points, while a hard-to-easy curriculum adds gains for spontaneous speech. These findings motivate reverse multi-stage fine-tuning (R-MFT), a training recipe that enables a parameter-efficient 244M Whisper model to match or exceed conventionally fine-tuned 769M counterparts. Representational analysis via CKA and SVD reveals effective schedules concentrate adaptation in the decoder, preserving the pre-trained encoder's acoustic geometry. We release the benchmark and models.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Vividh-ASR: Complexity-Tiered Benchmark and Optimization Dynamics for Robust Indic Speech Recognition Kush Juvekar ID , Kavya Manohar ID , Aditya Srinivas Menon ID , Arghya Bhattacharya ID , Kumarmanas Nethil ID Adalat AI, India {kush,kavya,dia,arghya,manas}@adalat.ai 6 2 0 2 3 1 ] . sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Fine-tuning multilingual ASR models like Whisper for low-resource languages often improves read speech but degrades spontaneous audio performancea phenomenon we term studio-bias. To diagnose this mismatch, we introduce Vividh-ASR, complexity-stratified benchmark for Hindi and Malayalam across four tiers: studio, broadcast, spontaneous, and synthetic noise. Through controlled study of learningrate timing and curriculum ordering, we find that early large parameter updates improve global WER by 12 absolute points, while hard-to-easy curriculum adds gains for spontaneous speech. These findings motivate reverse multi-stage fine-tuning (R-MFT), training recipe that enables parameter-efficient 244M Whisper model to match or exceed conventionally finetuned 769M counterparts. Representational analysis via CKA and SVD reveals effective schedules concentrate adaptation in the decoder, preserving the pre-trained encoders acoustic geometry. We release the benchmark and models. Index Terms: speech recognition, curriculum learning, Indic languages, fine-tuning 1. Introduction Large-scale weakly supervised pre-training has brought automatic speech recognition (ASR) close to human performance in high-resource languages [1, 2]. However, zero-shot word error rates (WER) for many Indic languages often exceed 100%. Fine-tuned models such as IndicWhisper [3] reduce this gap, but are trained primarily on studio-recorded read speech. Consequently, they perform well on clean audio yet degrade sharply on spontaneous conversational speecha failure mode we term studio-bias. Two conventions dominate current fine-tuning practice for models like Whisper. First, conservative learning rates (e.g., = 1e5) are commonly used to avoid catastrophic forgetting [4, 5] of the multilingual prior. Second, when curricula are employed, training typically follows an easy-to-hard progression [6] that gradually introduces noisier and more spontaneous speech. These heuristics implicitly assume that the pre-trained encoder already contains the acoustic structure necessary for low-resource target languages. In practice, however, adapting to the complex phonotactics and rapid articulation of spontaneous Dravidian and Indo-Aryan speech requires substantial early plasticity. Rather than relying on data scaling, we argue that adaptation efficiency is governed by two interacting factors: the timing of large parameter updates and the ordering of acoustic complexity. We systematically decouple these dynamics through controlled 2 2 factorial study, isolating the effects of learningrate timing and curriculum direction while holding the training Standard MFT (Easy-to-Hard) Stage 1 Tier (Studio) LR = 2e4 R-MFT (Ours) (Hard-to-Easy) Stage 1 Tier (Spontaneous) LR = 2e4 Stage 2 Tier (Broadcast) LR = 1e4 Stage 2 Tier (Broadcast) LR = 1e4 Stage 3 Tier C+A (Mix) LR = 1e Stage 3 Tier A+C (Mix) LR = 1e5 Figure 1: Comparison of training curricula. Both use identical decreasing LR schedules. R-MFT (right) places spontaneous data in the high-LR phase. data, model architecture, and optimizer configuration constant. Our findings challenge the status quo: we demonstrate that reversing standard heuristicsapplying high-magnitude updates initially on the hardest datayields drastic WER improvements on identical data distributions, while conservative initialization traps the model in sub-optimal basin. To better understand these dynamics, we analyze how different optimization schedules reshape internal model representations using centered kernel alignment (CKA) [7] and singular value decomposition (SVD). This suggests that effective schedules concentrate most adaptation within the decoder while largely preserving the encoders pre-trained acoustic geometry. We make two primary contributions: 1. Vividh-ASR Benchmark. diagnostic benchmark for Hindi and Malayalam that stratifies evaluation by acoustic complexity: studio (Tier A), broadcast (Tier B), spontaneous (Tier C), and synthetic noise (Tier D). Unlike domain-based benchmarks, this structure isolates precisely where models fail along the complexity axis. 2. Reverse multi-stage fine-tuning (R-MFT). training recipe that pairs spontaneous-first data ordering with high initial learning rates. We release parameter-efficient 244M Whisper model trained with this recipe, demonstrating its efficacy on spontaneous Indic speech. 2. Related Work Indic ASR corpora and benchmarks. Open-source datasets for Indian languages have expanded rapidly. Kathbath [8] provides large-scale read speech, Shrutilipi [9] broadcast news transcriptions, and Indic Voices [10] crowdsourced spontaneous speech. Benchmarks such as Vistaar [3] evaluate models across multiple domains, analogous to ESB for English [11]. Vividh-ASR complements these resources by introducing complexity-stratified evaluation axis that isolates performance across acoustic difficulty rather than domains. Curriculum learning for ASR. Curriculum learning is widely used in ASR training, typically exposing models to progressively noisy data [12]. Anti-curriculum and self-paced strategies have been explored in other domains [13], but their role in adapting large pre-trained speech models remains underexplored. Our work studies how curriculum direction interacts with optimization schedules during fine-tuning. Mechanics of adaptation. Adapting multilingual models like Whisper [1] to low-resource languages poses distinct challenge: learning new phonotactics without degrading the pre-trained acoustic representation. Prior work has analyzed the internal representations of frozen speech models [14, 15], but the fine-tuning process itself is typically evaluated solely through downstream WER. We bridge this gap by examining how fine-tuning schedules physically reshape internal representations using centered kernel alignment (CKA) [7, 16] and singular value decomposition (SVD), linking empirical optimization choices to shifts in model geometry. 3. The Vividh-ASR Benchmark Vividh-ASR is diagnostic benchmark organized by acoustic and prosodic complexity rather than by domain. It targets Hindi and Malayalam, representing the Indo-Aryan and Dravidian language families respectively, and aggregates data from Kathbath [8], Shrutilipi [9], Indic Voices [10], FLEURS [17], and additional publicly available corpora [18]. 3.1. Tier Definitions Tier (Studio): Scripted, read speech in controlled environments. Clear articulation, standard pronunciation, deliberate pace. Serves as performance ceiling reference. Tier (Broadcast): Read speech from news broadcasts. Clean audio but significantly higher speech rate than Tier A, testing temporal modeling. Tier (Spontaneous): Crowdsourced, unscripted recordings with disfluencies, varying prosody, background noise, and non-professional hardware. The primary bottleneck for real-world Indic ASR. Tier (Noise): Tier audio augmented with synthetic noise profiles (babble, music, environmental). Held out from training; used exclusively for zero-shot evaluation of acoustic robustness transfer. 3.2. Data Statistics Table 1 summarizes the corpus. The distribution is intentionally weighted toward Tier C, reflecting our focus on closing the spontaneous-speech performance gap. 4. Methodology Standard Whisper fine-tuning relies on conservative learning rates (1e5) under the assumption that large updates will destroy the pre-trained priors [19]. However, when adapting to low-resource languages with complex phonotactics, the model Table 1: Data distribution in hours. The corpus is intentionally weighted toward spontaneous speech (Tier C). Tier is evaluation-only. Tier Train (h) Val (h) Eval (h) (Studio) (Broadcast) (Spontaneous) (Noise) Total Mal 182.2 200.0 512.5 894.7 Hi 272.1 1359.91 558.65 2190. Mal 10.2 12.6 20.1 42.9 Hi 12.0 13.2 22.1 47. Mal 4.28 6.33 9.62 3.08 23.31 Hi 12.50 6.82 17.43 3.00 39. must escape loss basin shaped by the pretraining distribution. We hypothesize that data scaling alone is insufficient; optimization plasticity (learning rate) and data ordering (curriculum) must be manipulated systematically. 4.1. Controlled Factorial Design To disentangle the effects of optimization plasticity and data ordering, we designed controlled 2 2 factorial ablation over two axes: 1. Learning Rate (LR) Timing: We compare decreasing schedule (2e4 1e4 1e5) against an increasing schedule (1e5 1e4 2e4). This isolates when the model receives large parameter updates. 2. Curriculum Direction: We compare an easy-to-hard ordering (Tier Tier Tier C) against hard-to-easy ordering (Tier Tier Tier A). This experimental design allows us to isolate whether acoustic robustness is primarily function of the data seen, or the plasticity of the model when it sees that data. 4.2. Reverse Multi-Stage Fine-Tuning (R-MFT) Based on the empirical findings of this factorial study (detailed in Section 5), we propose Reverse Multi-Stage Fine-Tuning (R-MFT). R-MFT pairs the optimal conditions from our ablation: high initial learning rate to break out of the pre-trained basin, coupled with spontaneous-first (hard-to-easy) curriculum. The recipe consists of three stages: 1. Stage 1 (Spontaneous, LR = 2e4): Tier data. The highest-plasticity phase encounters the highest-complexity data, explicitly building robustness to disfluencies, noise, and varying prosody. 2. Stage 2 (Broadcast, LR = 1e4): Tier data. Refines temporal modeling for rapid speech. 3. Stage 3 (Consolidation, LR = 1e5): 1:1 mixture by duration of Tier and Tier C. This multi-objective stage acts as regularizer, recovering any spontaneous performance degraded during Stage 2 while optimizing studio precision. Table 2: Ablation matrix. Conditions 36 form 2 2 factorial over LR direction and curriculum direction. # 1 2 3 4 5 6 Condition LR Schedule Single-stage, low LR Single-stage, high LR 1e5 cosine 2e4 cosine Curriculum All tiers mixed All tiers mixed Standard MFT R-MFT (ours) 2e4 1e4 1e5 2e4 1e4 1e5 C+A A+C Increasing LR, EH Increasing LR, HE 1e5 1e4 2e4 1e5 1e4 2e4 C+A A+C 4.3. Implementation Details We evaluate on Whisper-small (244M) and Whisper-medium (769M). All training stages utilize AdamW with weight decay 0.1. Each stage uses linear warmup for the first 10% of steps followed by cosine annealing. We train with batch size of 128 and grdient checkpointing to reduce memory used while training. Each stage trains for few epochs. Tier data is strictly held out from all training and validation splits. Models are trained using HuggingFace Transformers on NVIDIA H100 GPUs. 5. Results 5.1. Learning Rate Effect Figure 2 shows training loss for the Malayalam Whispermedium model (representative; Hindi and Whisper-small exhibit identical trends). The conservative LR (1e5) plateaus within the first 7K steps at loss an order of magnitude higher than the 2e4 schedule, consistent with the hypothesis that the pre-trained prior creates deep, narrow basin from which small gradients cannot escape. Figure 2: Training loss: high LR (2e4) vs. low LR (1e5) for Malayalam Whisper-medium. The low-LR schedule plateaus prematurely. Identical trends hold for Hindi and Whisper-small. 5.2. Overview Table 4 shows both high-LR strategies (R-MFT and Singlestage high LR) vastly outperform the conservative single-stage low-LR baseline (77.79%/25.25% global WER). While R-MFT yields the best results for Malayalam (39.36%), we find that Single-stage high LR is slightly superior for Hindi (16.67% vs 18.82% for R-MFT). Both methods significantly exceed the performance of IndicWhisper. This suggests that while the hard-toeasy curriculum is optimal for the more complex phonotactics of Malayalam, simply escaping the low-LR local minima is the primary requirement for Hindi. 5.3. Effect of Learning Rate Timing Comparing schedule directions in Table 3 reveals that LR timing is the primary determinant of performance. In Malayalam, starting with low LR locks the model into representational trajectory that later high-LR stages cannot reverse, resulting in consistent 13-point penalty across curricula. This locking effect is most severe under R-MFT, where low-LR start leads to initial catastrophic failure from which the model never fully recovers. Hindi mirrors this requirement for early plasticity. Regardless of curriculum direction, high-to-low schedules converge to 18.8% global WER (improving from 28.49% initially for standard and 22.71% for reverse). These results confirm that Table 3: Impact of LR Timing vs. Curriculum Direction on Final Global WER (Malayalam, 769M). Decreasing LR (High Low) consistently avoids the sub-optimal basins that trap increasing-LR schedules.

sections.title: Gap
sections.content: 42.25 55.21 +12.96 39.35 51.86 +12.51 high-magnitude updates must be applied at the outset to escape the pre-trained prior; delayed high-energy updates are insufficient to exit sub-optimal representational basins formed during conservative initialization. 5.4. Effect of Curriculum Ordering Holding the LR schedule constant isolates the impact of data ordering. In Malayalam, hard-to-easy ordering (R-MFT, 39.35% WER) consistently outperforms the standard easy-to-hard MFT (42.25% WER). While this 3-point gain is smaller than the 13-point timing effect, it is critical for robustness: exposing the model to the highest-complexity spontaneous data (Tier C) during its initial high-plasticity phase allows the decoder to adapt to disfluencies and non-professional acoustics before refining on cleaner tiers. In contrast, Hindi results show convergence to 18.8% global WER for both curriculum directions. This suggests that while high-energy initialization is mandatory requirement for both languages to escape the pre-trained prior, the specific ordering of acoustic complexity provides specialized advantage for the more challenging phonotactic and prosodic landscape of Malayalam. 5.5. Parameter Scale The R-MFT recipe enables remarkable parameter efficiency. Our 244M R-MFT (Small) model achieves 44.41% (Mal) and 21.41% (Hi) global WER. This outperforms the much larger 769M Single-stage low-LR baseline by 33.38 and 3.84 absolute points respectively, despite having 1/3 the parameter capacity. Furthermore, the 244M model exceeds the 769M IndicWhisper baseline in both languages, demonstrating that hard-data first optimization trajectory is more effective than raw model scaling for robust Indic ASR. As shown, the R-MFT recipe utilizes its parameter capacity highly efficiently. However, macroscopic WER improvements alone do not explain why conservative fine-tuning fails to generalize to complex acoustics, nor how high-LR schedules safely modify the network. To understand the underlying mechanics of this adaptation, we must examine the internal geometry of the models. 6. Analysis We hypothesize that successful adaptation to low-resource Indic phonotactics requires structural asymmetry: learning new linguistic priors in the decoder while preserving the pre-trained Table 4: WER (%) across Vividh-ASR tiers for Malayalam (Mal) and Hindi (Hi). Best per-column in bold. : uses less Tier training data than our models (see text).

sections.content: Hi IndicWhisper Single-stage, low LR Single-stage, high LR Standard MFT R-MFT (Medium) R-MFT (Small) 769M 769M 769M 769M 769M 244M 33.01 55. 27.31 33.56 31.66 36.49 16.20 24.01 12.38 16.41 16.09 19.16 33.11 78.64 30.48 32.75 31.66 35.05 11.62 16. 11.33 10.91 10.11 11.49 66.09 82.37 50.30 51.03 46.18 53.74 39.87 30.62 22.99 24.91 24.91 27.34 48.07 82. 50.78 51.51 45.73 48.04 14.99 21.60 14.05 16.25 17.27 20.97 48.64 77.79 40.39 42.25 39.36 44.41 25.01 25. 16.67 18.81 18.82 21.41 encoders acoustic invariance. To test this, we trace representational shifts relative to the base Whisper model using four complementary metrics: (i) relative L2 weight displacement (θ) to measure optimization plasticity, (ii) Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to verify preservation of activation geometry, (iii) exact Optimal Transport (Wasserstein-1 EMD) [20] to capture distribution drift, and (iv) Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to detect structural fragmentation. 6.1. Encoder vs. Decoder Adaptation Table 5 reveals critical structural asymmetry that distinguishes our high-LR methods. Successful adaptation is characterized by substantial parameter displacement concentrated almost exclusively in the decoder (mean θ = 0.122 for R-MFT). This is mirrored by significant shift in decoder activation distributions (EMD = 0.069), indicating that the model is actively re-mapping the linguistic prior. Crucially, the encoders pre-trained geometry remains invariant. For both Baseline-High and R-MFT, Encoder CKA remains perfect (1.000) with near-zero EMD. This demonstrates that an aggressive initial step size (2e4) does not destroy the encoder; rather, it provides the necessary gradient energy for the decoder to escape the pre-trained basin while the encoder remains anchored to its robust acoustic features. Conversely, the conservative low-LR baseline fails to generate sufficient displacement (θ 0.01) to achieve any meaningful adaptation. Table 5: Layer-wise representational shifts relative to the base Whisper-medium model. Model vs. Base CKA EMD θ (L2) Enc Dec Enc Dec Enc Dec 0.775 IndicWhisper 1.000 Baseline-Low LR Baseline-High LR 1. R-MFT Stage 1 R-MFT Final 1.000 1.000 0.993 0.999 0.999 0.999 0.999 0.605 0.000 0.000 0.000 0. 0.467 0.168 0.100 0.072 0.069 0.025 0.010 0.080 0.073 0.076 0.016 0.018 0.123 0.112 0. 6.2. Spectral Signatures of Studio-Bias IndicWhisper, fine-tuned predominantly on read and broadcast speech, exhibits fundamentally different structural signature. Despite lower overall weight displacement than R-MFT (θ = 0.025), it severely disrupts the pre-trained encoders geometry, dropping CKA to 0.775. We verify this shift via rank expansion  (Table 6)  . While the base model and R-MFT maintain compact Encoder Effective Rank (ζ 14), IndicWhisper expands this to ζ = 25. Although differing training distributions preclude strictly causal comparison, this expansion suggests that clean-acoustic adaptation overwrites the encoders robust feature space with studiospecific nuances. This over-parameterization correlates with the steep degradation observed on spontaneous speech (66.09% WER), as the model loses the generalized acoustic invariance of the pre-trained prior. In contrast, our R-MFT scheduledriven by hard-to-easy curriculum and high initial learning ratesachieves superior adaptation entirely through decoder realignment, leaving the foundational acoustic robustness of the encoder intact. Table 6: Singular value decomposition (SVD) analysis of encoder and decoder activations. Model Effective Rank Dec Enc Whisper base IndicWhisper Baseline-High LR R-MFT Final 14 25 14 14 9 11 9 Tail S1 Enc .0134 .0443 .0134 .0134 Dec .0018 .0045 .0019 .0017 7. Conclusion We introduced Vividh-ASR, complexity-tiered benchmark designed to diagnose studio-bias in Indic speech recognition. Through 2 2 factorial study on Hindi and Malayalam, we demonstrated that optimization plasticity dominates curriculum ordering: early large parameter updates yield 12 absolute WER points of improvement, while hard-to-easy curriculum provides further gains on spontaneous speech. Crucially, later high-learning-rate stages cannot recover performance lost during conservative, low-LR initialization. Motivated by these dynamics, we proposed reverse multistage fine-tuning (R-MFT), enabling 244M Whisper model to match or exceed conventionally fine-tuned 769M counterparts. CKA and SVD analyses confirm this strategy adapts the decoder to target-language phonotactics while preserving the encoders robust acoustic geometry. Future work will extend Vividh-ASR to additional languages and investigate if these optimization dynamics generalize beyond Whisper to self-supervised and Conformer-based models [21]. Finally, given our mechanistic findings regarding encoder invariance, we specifically aim to explore the efficacy of selective encoder freezing as regularization strategy to further mitigate studiobias.
